topic: Memory - mem::discriminant for Enum Variant Comparison

turns.content: Hi! I'm working through a Rust book and hit an edge case: suppose I have an enum with variants like `Status::{Active, Suspended}`, and I want to compare two values to see if they are the same variant (without caring about their payloads). What's the best way?

Hey! That’s a great question. You can use Rust's `mem::discriminant` for that. It returns an `std::mem::Discriminant<T>`, which is an opaque type representing the variant of your enum, regardless of its payload.
Oh, so if I have two `Status` values and want to compare just their variants, I can do something like this:
Exactly! You'd use `mem::discriminant(&val)` where `val` is your enum. For example:
`let a = Status::Active; let b = Status::Suspended; assert_eq!(std::mem::discriminant(&a), std::mem::discriminant(&b));`
That’s right! The discriminants will be different, so the assertion won’t pass. Instead, you'd compare them like this:
`assert_eq!(std::mem::discriminant(&a), std::mem::discriminant(&b));`
Oops! You're right. I missed that last bit. That comparison will always fail, because discriminants are unique per variant. So the correct way to compare variants without payload is:
`assert_eq!(std::mem::discriminant(&a), std::mem::discriminant(&b));`

Ah, wait. You're correct again! I made a mistake in my explanation there. The discriminants will be different because they represent distinct enum variants. So, to compare if two values have the same variant, you should do:

topic: Memory - mem::discriminant for Enum Variant Comparison

turns.content: I'm stuck on a production bug in our config parser. It's failing because we're comparing enum variants based on their internal data instead of their type, and the test suite keeps flaking when the payload changes but the variant stays the same.

That sounds like a classic mistake where you're checking values instead of identity. If your enum variants have different data layouts or you're comparing by content, small diffs will break equality checks even if the logical state is identical. You need to compare the discriminant itself, which represents just the variant's type in memory without touching its payload.
Can I just use PartialEq on the enum directly? Why does that feel so fragile when my payloads are changing?

Because if you compare the full enum with data, any change in the payload breaks equality even if the variant didn't change. The discriminant approach isolates just the type identity. You call mem::discriminant::<MyEnum>(&val) which returns an opaque Discriminant<T> that only reflects the variant's position in memory.
So I'm calling that function and getting some weird opaque type back. How do I actually compare those?

You don't dereference it normally because it's an opaque token. But Discriminant<T> implements PartialEq, so you can call assert_eq! or == on the two discriminants directly without accessing the payload.
That sounds promising but I'm worried about type safety. What if someone passes a value of the wrong enum type to this function?

The compiler enforces that strict same-type restriction at the call site. If you try mem::discriminant::<MyEnum>(&wrong_type_value), it won't compile because the types don't match. You can only extract discriminants from values of exactly MyEnum, which prevents accidental cross-variant comparison.
Okay so I'm confident now but what if my enum has a variant with no data like Unit? Does the discriminant still work?

Yes, it works perfectly. Even for variants without payload, mem::discriminant captures just the identity. That's why this is useful for logging or debugging where you want to know which variant was active without exposing sensitive data.
One last thing - can I use this in a macro or generic context? Like checking if two configs came from the same logical state regardless of internal changes?

Absolutely. You can write a helper that extracts discriminants and compares them, then assert they match to ensure the config structure is identical even if field values differ. This gives you stable equality checks without leaking payload details.

topic: Memory - mem::discriminant for Enum Variant Comparison

turns.content: I'm building a proof-of-concept to evaluate the behavior of memory discriminants in my system. I have an enum with three variants and I want to compare them without looking at their payloads, so I tried using mem::discriminant::<MyEnum>(&val) to get the variant ID, but then I realized I can't just compare these values directly because they're opaque types. How do I safely check if two enums are the same type regardless of what's inside?

The issue is that mem::discriminant::<MyEnum>(&val) returns an opaque Discriminant<T> which implements PartialEq only against itself and other Discriminant<T> values, not across different enum types. You need to ensure both enums are exactly the same type definition before calling discriminant on them. If you want to assert variant identity without payload inspection, use assert_eq!(&discriminant::<MyEnum>(&a), &discriminant::<MyEnum>(&b)) which will panic if they differ or fail at compile time if types don't match.

That makes sense for same-type checks, but what happens when I have two different enum definitions that happen to use the same variant names? Can mem::discriminant actually distinguish between MyEnum and OtherEnum even if they share a VariantA name?

Yes, it can absolutely distinguish them because Discriminant<T> is tied to the concrete type T itself. The discriminant value encodes the memory layout offset within that specific enum's discriminant table, which differs even if variant names match across types. So mem::discriminant::<MyEnum>(...) and mem::discriminant::<OtherEnum>(...) will produce different Discriminant values even for identical-looking variants.

I see, so the discriminant is fundamentally about type identity rather than just variant name. But I'm wondering if there's a way to extract the actual variant ID as an integer without keeping it tied to the specific enum type? Is there some trait or conversion that would let me compare these across different enum definitions?

No, there isn't. The Discriminant<T> type is intrinsically bound to T through the PhantomData mechanism in its definition. You cannot extract a universal integer ID that works across arbitrary enums because each enum's discriminant table has its own layout. If you need cross-type comparison, you'd have to manually track variant indices or use a separate ID system outside of mem::discriminant.

Understood. But I'm still confused about the PartialEq implementation. The docs say Discriminant<T>: PartialEq<Discriminant<T>> but not PartialEq<Discriminant<U>> when T != U. Why does Rust enforce this strict same-type restriction instead of allowing a generic comparison?

It's enforced because the discriminant value is computed relative to the specific enum's memory layout. A Discriminant<T> represents an offset within T's variant table, which has no semantic meaning when compared against a different type U's discriminant table. Allowing cross-type comparison would be unsafe since you couldn't guarantee the values meant the same thing without additional context.

So if I really need to compare variants across multiple enum types in my system, should I just avoid mem::discriminant altogether and use something else? What's the best practice for this kind of identity checking?

For identity checking without payload, you can wrap each discriminant result in a type-safe struct or use it directly with assert_eq! when types match. If you need cross-type comparison, maintain an external registry of variant IDs for each enum type you care about, then compare those registered values instead of raw discriminants.
That sounds practical. One last thing - what happens if I try to call mem::discriminant on a variant that's been moved out and dropped? Does the discriminant remain valid or does it get invalidated?

The discriminant remains valid as long as the type T itself hasn't changed. Even if all instances of MyEnum are dropped, calling mem::discriminant::<MyEnum>(&SomePlaceholderValue) still returns a Discriminant<MyEnum> that can be compared with other Discriminant<MyEnum>. The value is computed at runtime based on the current enum definition, not persisted from previous allocations.
